context: Current closing price of Equinix (EQIX): $1052.98 USD as of 2026-04-15.
justification_text:
My point estimates reflect a modest upward drift across time horizons, starting near 1058 for the one-day horizon and gradually rising to around 1075 at the one-month mark. This trajectory suggests I was assuming some mild bullish momentum or mean-reversion tendency in whatever asset or index this represents, combined with a baseline assumption of low near-term volatility. The one-day estimate is anchored most heavily to recent price action and short-term momentum signals, while the one-week and one-month estimates incorporate a slightly longer-term trend component, possibly reflecting macroeconomic conditions, earnings expectations, or broader market sentiment that was mildly constructive at the time of the forecast.

The confidence interval widths widen progressively across horizons, which is a standard feature of probabilistic forecasting — uncertainty compounds over time. At one day, the 90% interval spans roughly 76 points (1020 to 1096), reflecting relatively contained intraday or overnight risk. By one week, this expands to 150 points, and by one month, the interval covers 270 points (940 to 1210). These widths were likely calibrated based on historical realized volatility for the underlying asset, with the assumption that daily volatility roughly scales with the square root of time. Liquidity conditions, recent volatility regimes, and the absence of any known near-term catalysts at the time would also have shaped how tightly or loosely I drew these bands.

Several specific risks could push outcomes well outside these stated intervals. On the downside, a sudden deterioration in macroeconomic data — such as a surprise in inflation readings, an unexpected central bank policy shift, or a credit event in a systemically important institution — could easily breach the lower bounds. Geopolitical shocks or a liquidity crisis could be particularly severe at the one-month horizon. On the upside, a strongly positive policy surprise, better-than-expected corporate earnings, or a broad risk-on rotation could drive prices through the upper bound, especially over the one-month window where there is more time for such catalysts to develop and accumulate.

It is also worth acknowledging that my stated 90% intervals are themselves uncertain — they represent my best attempt at calibration but are not guaranteed to contain the true outcome 90% of the time in practice. Model misspecification, regime changes, or tail events that don't appear in historical data (so-called black swans) represent a structural risk that any probabilistic forecast carries. Users should treat these intervals as informed estimates rather than precise probabilistic guarantees, and they should monitor for new information that would warrant updating these forecasts materially.
